name: Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail
name_original: Pedra de les Bruixes, El Dau, Santa Esteve de Tavèrnoles, Santa Magdalena de Conangle – Ruta Circular
description: Hike the moderate 7.4-mile Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail to explore ancient rock engravings and enjoy 360-degree panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Tavèrnoles offers diverse landscapes, transitioning from cultivated plains to mountainous, forested areas with pines, oaks, and holm oaks. The region is shaped by the Ter River and provides access to the Sau reservoir. Hikers can find trails leading to panoramic viewpoints and natural features like the Minyona Waterfall. Best hiking trails around Tavèrnoles The most popular hiking route is Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail, a 7.4 miles (11.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 27 minutes to complete. This moderate route allows exploration of ancient rock engravings and offers extensive panoramic views. Another top favourite among local hikers is Minyona Waterfall Viewpoint loop from Tavèrnoles, a moderate 4.9 miles (7.9 km) path. This trail leads to the picturesque Minyona Waterfall, a notable natural highlight in the area. Local hikers also love the Ter River and Tartera – View towards the Monastery loop from Tavèrnoles, a 9.8 miles (15.8 km) trail leading through the Ter River valley with views towards the Romanesque Monastery of Sant Pere de Casserres, often completed in about 4 hours 40 minutes. Hiking around Tavèrnoles is defined by cultivated plains, mountainous forests, and the Ter River, offering panoramic viewpoints. The network provides options for various ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging treks. The routes in Tavèrnoles are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 400 reviews. More than 3,400 hikers have used komoot to explore Tavèrnoles's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Tavèrnoles?
A: Tavèrnoles offers a selection of around 20 hiking trails, catering to various skill levels. These routes traverse diverse landscapes, from cultivated plains to mountainous forests, providing access to panoramic viewpoints and natural features.

Q: Are there easy hiking options suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Tavèrnoles has 7 easy hiking routes, making it suitable for beginners or families looking for a relaxed outdoor experience. The municipality also offers fixed orientation circuits, which are ideal for family activities.

Q: Are there any trails that feature waterfalls?
A: Yes, the area is known for its picturesque waterfalls. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31984118" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Minyona Waterfall Viewpoint loop from Tavèrnoles&lt;/a&gt;, which leads to the notable Minyona Waterfall. Other natural highlights in the broader region include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/576239" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sallent Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/380370" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Foradada Waterfall and Mill&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of views can I expect on the trails around Tavèrnoles?
A: Hikers can expect stunning panoramic views, particularly from spots like Sant Feliuet de Savassona, which offers 360-degree vistas. Another excellent viewpoint is Puig del Far, accessible via trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40337389" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puig del Far – Viewpoint Before Puig del Far loop&lt;/a&gt;. You can also find views of the Ter River valley and the Romanesque Monastery of Sant Pere de Casserres on routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10471345" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ter River and Tartera – View towards the Monastery loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural sites to see while hiking?
A: Absolutely. Tavèrnoles trails often incorporate historical and cultural elements. You can visit the ancient rock engravings and the hermitage of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4382081" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sant Feliuet de Savassona&lt;/a&gt;, or explore prehistoric sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5151750" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pedra de les Bruixes Rock Engravings&lt;/a&gt;. The Romanesque church of Sant Esteve and the castle and church of Sant Pere de Savassona are also notable points of interest.

Q: Are the hiking trails in Tavèrnoles suitable for dogs?
A: Many trails in Tavèrnoles are generally dog-friendly, allowing you to enjoy the diverse landscapes with your canine companion. It's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or livestock, and to follow local regulations.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about Tavèrnoles?
A: The hiking routes in Tavèrnoles are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 400 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, the stunning panoramic views, and the well-maintained paths that lead to natural and historical attractions.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Tavèrnoles?
A: Yes, Tavèrnoles offers several circular routes, perfect for hikers who prefer to start and end their journey in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31984118" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Minyona Waterfall Viewpoint loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10471345" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ter River and Tartera – View towards the Monastery loop&lt;/a&gt;, which provide diverse scenery and points of interest.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Tavèrnoles?
A: Tavèrnoles offers enjoyable hiking opportunities throughout much of the year due to its varied climate. Spring and autumn are particularly pleasant, with mild temperatures and vibrant natural colors. Summer can be warm, but shaded forest trails provide relief, while winter offers a different, often quieter, experience.

Q: Is there parking available near the trailheads in Tavèrnoles?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around Tavèrnoles, particularly near popular trailheads and key attractions. Specific parking information can often be found on individual route descriptions or local tourism resources.

Q: Can I reach Tavèrnoles hiking trails using public transport?
A: While Tavèrnoles is primarily accessed by car, some public transport options may connect to nearby towns. For specific routes and schedules, it's advisable to check local bus services or regional transport links that might serve the area, though direct access to all trailheads via public transport might be limited.
tips:
It is built at the foot of the Castle of Savassona at an altitude of about 611 m and has an aesthetic similar to that of the surroundings, which must have been built by the masters themselves, such as Sant Feliuet, Sant Esteve de Tavèrnoles, Santa Margarida d'Ardola, Casserres, etc.

The work must have been completed around 1069, when he received a bequest destined for his dedication, which was made in 1078. The bishop of Vic ceded the chapel to R. Folc, who owned it until 1086, when he died in hands of the Saracens. Then the church passed into the hands of the Vigo canon.

The church of Sant Pere is a suffragan of Sant Esteve de Tavèrnoles.

The history of the baptismal font is linked to that of the church, which was built around 1069.

It is one of the best preserved Romanesque churches in the Osona region.

It was built by the viscounts of Osona around 1069 in one of their fiefdoms and was recognized nine years later by the bishop of Vic to viscount Ramon Folc, who, when he died at the hands of the Saracens in 1086, gave it up together with the church of Sant Pere in Vigata canonical.

As the majority of churches in the region in the Baroque period were enlarged and transformed, so, as the inscription indicates, around 1728 it was raised, side chapels were built, one of which was abolished during the restoration and open the western portal. Despite everything, the Romanesque beauty of the temple can still be admired.

The first tombstone belongs to the tomb of Miquel Galles, rector of the parish of Tavernoles, who died on August 20, 1839.

The second corresponds to Jacinto Verdaguer, who died in 1868. It is probably Jacint Verdaguer i Ordeix, uncle and godfather of the poet from Folgueroles.

The other two tombstones correspond to the inhabitants of the neighboring houses: the Cos and the Ruquet.

The hermitage of Santa Magdalena is of Romanesque origin although over time it has undergone modifications. It was built around 1231. In 1304 a small community of Augustinian nuns settled here, who had their headquarters here until 1450. From then until 1960, the hermitage remained in the care of hermits, until the Carmelite cloister was moved Vic's barefoot

The Stone of the Witches is a large natural stone with a large central crack that divides it into two halves. On this stone you can see a lot of medieval engravings - more than 50 crosses, 9 horseshoes, 6 bowls, various circles and other figures. According to legend, these marks were made by witches who were at odds with the Baron of Savassona. It seems that the Baron of Savassona was connected to the Inquisition and the witches wanted to send him a warning.

It is a large rock in the shape of a fallen cube. Remains from the Chalcolithic period to the Iberian period, mainly pottery, have been found in excavations carried out around the Dau in 1975.

It is a place of practice of Búlder (adaptation of the English boulder), as it can be appreciated by the magnesium stains.

It is one of the most important sites in the group. It was a place used in the Iron Age as a burial place. Among the excavation findings is a well-preserved skeleton. During the excavation, houses built on the rock of the Middle Ages were found. You can clearly see the ledge that runs along the rock and where the beams of the construction were laid.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail?
faqs.answer: The main circular route to the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Hermitage is generally considered easy and suitable for families and children. However, some sources describe the trail as moderate, likely referring to longer variations or the short, steep ascent to the hermitage via a medieval stone staircase. While the komoot tour is rated moderate, the core experience is accessible.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this trail?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter varied terrain, including pleasant forest paths and significant rocky areas. The route features impressive, often enormous, rocks, some with ancient Neolithic engravings. Be aware that some sections, especially around the hermitage, can be rocky and potentially slippery, particularly in winter.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail is rich in history and natural beauty. Key highlights include the pre-Romanesque Sant Feliuet de Savassona Hermitage, which offers breathtaking 360-degree panoramic views. You'll also discover ancient rock engravings like the Pedra de les Bruixes Rock Engravings and The Stone of Sacrifices, as well as the unique El Dau Boulder. Other notable sites include Sant Pere de Savassona, Sant Esteve de Tavèrnoles Church, and Ermita de Santa Magdalena de Conangle.

faqs.question: Is the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary circular route is generally considered family-friendly. However, caution is advised with small children near the hermitage due to unprotected cliff areas. The ascent to the hermitage via the medieval staircase is short but can be steep.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail is generally considered suitable for dogs. Always ensure your dog is under control, especially in areas with wildlife or other hikers, and check for any local leash regulations.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer is also good, but it can get warm, so an early start is recommended. In winter, some rocky sections might be slippery, but the views can be stunning.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Les Guilleríes-Savassona natural area?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to access the trails within the Espai Natural de les Guilleríes-Savassona, where this route is located. However, always respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Trail?
faqs.answer: Parking is typically available near the starting points in the municipality of Tavèrnoles. Specific parking areas are usually signposted, often close to the main access roads leading into the natural area. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends, as spots can fill up.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with other hiking routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other official trails. You'll find sections that are part of the Ruta del Ter, El Meridià Verd, Camí Oliba, and the Vic-Sant Pere de Casserres route, among others. This offers opportunities to extend your hike if you wish.

faqs.question: What makes the views from the hermitage so special?
faqs.answer: From the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Hermitage, you are treated to expansive 360-degree panoramic views. These vistas encompass the Montseny massif, the Plana de Vic, the Collsacabra mountains, the distant Pyrenees, and the winding Ter River, offering a truly spectacular perspective of the Catalan landscape.

faqs.question: Are there any unique historical or archaeological features on the trail?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. The trail passes through an ancient Iberian settlement, and the Sant Feliuet de Savassona Hermitage itself stands on this historic ground. You'll see anthropomorphic tombs and cisterns carved directly into the living rock, alongside the Neolithic rock engravings on colossal stones like the Pedra de les Bruixes, adding a deep sense of history to your hike.
